description:
You are a network engineer who has been asked to diagnose API-level
    connectivity on a freshly-provisioned host that (so far) has no
    monitoring in place.  
    Management wants a **single, self-contained log file** proving that
    three specific HTTP transactions work (or fail) exactly as expected.
    A junior colleague already copied one reference response file into
    /home/user/mock_api/ok.json but left the rest of the job to you.

    Your assignment

    1. Spin up a minimal, local web service that serves the directory
       /home/user/mock_api on TCP port 9090.  
       • It must stay running long enough for you to test against it.  
       • You are NOT allowed to run anything as root.  
       • Use only utilities that are part of a standard Ubuntu/Fedora
         workstation install (python3, busybox httpd, etc. are fine).

    2. Create the directory /home/user/connectivity_logs  
       (it must exist at the end of the task).

    3. Run **exactly two** HTTP GET requests with curl, executed in
       parallel (for instance through “xargs -P2”, subshell
       backgrounding, or any other mechanism that launches both
       requests concurrently).  
       The URLs are:  
       a. http://127.0.0.1:9090/ok.json  
       b. http://127.0.0.1:9090/missing.json   —(this file does *not*
         exist, so a 404 is expected).

    4. For each request capture only four data points and write them into
       the single file  
       /home/user/connectivity_logs/api_test_results.log  
       **in the *exact* pipe-delimited format shown below**.  The file
       must contain one header line followed by one data line per URL:

       TIMESTAMP|URL|STATUS_CODE|RESULT  
       2023-03-02T15:04:05Z|http://127.0.0.1:9090/ok.json|200|SUCCESS  
       2023-03-02T15:04:05Z|http://127.0.0.1:9090/missing.json|404|FAILURE  

       Details and constraints:  
       • TIMESTAMP must be the current UTC time in RFC 3339/ISO-8601
         format (yyyy-mm-ddThh:mm:ssZ).  
       • URL is the literal request URL.  
       • STATUS_CODE is the integer HTTP status code returned by curl
         (200, 404, …).  
       • RESULT is the word “SUCCESS” if STATUS_CODE is 200, otherwise
         “FAILURE”.  
       • The header line must appear first and be spelled exactly
         as shown.  
       • The order of the two data lines is not important, but **both**
         must be present.  
       • No extra columns, no extra spaces, and each line must be
         terminated by a single newline “\n”.

       A quick visual example (your timestamps will differ):

       TIMESTAMP|URL|STATUS_CODE|RESULT
       2023-05-01T12:20:33Z|http://127.0.0.1:9090/ok.json|200|SUCCESS
       2023-05-01T12:20:33Z|http://127.0.0.1:9090/missing.json|404|FAILURE

    5. The log file is the only artefact the automated grader will
       inspect, so ensure it is written exactly once, in the exact path,
       with the exact formatting rules above.

    6. Do not shut down the web service until *after* the log file is
       written (otherwise curl will fail).

    Verification checklist (you do not have to output this list, it is
    just for you):  
       • /home/user/mock_api/ok.json exists and is reachable on port 9090  
       • /home/user/connectivity_logs exists  
       • /home/user/connectivity_logs/api_test_results.log exists  
       • api_test_results.log contains 3 lines (1 header + 2 data lines)  
       • The ok.json line shows status 200 and “SUCCESS”  
       • The missing.json line shows status 404 and “FAILURE”

    Hints (optional reading):  
       • python3 -m http.server 9090 -d /home/user/mock_api is usually
         the quickest way to expose a directory.  
       • curl “-w” and “-o /dev/null” flags can capture status codes
         cleanly.  
       • xargs with “-P <number>” is an easy way to fire off curl
         processes in parallel without going deep into shell
         backgrounding.  
       • Remember to use date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ for UTC timestamps.

    Once you are convinced everything is correct, end your terminal
    session.  The grading system will take it from there.
truth:
<!-- Pre-existing artefacts -->
    1. Directory /home/user/mock_api already exists (permissions 755).
    2. File /home/user/mock_api/ok.json already exists with **exact**
       contents:
       {"status":"ok"}
       (single trailing newline \n).

    <!-- Expected end-state for automatic grading -->
    A. Directory /home/user/connectivity_logs must exist (permissions
       755 or more lenient).
    B. File /home/user/connectivity_logs/api_test_results.log must exist.
       Its content must satisfy *all* of the following regular-expression
       rules (POSIX ERE syntax):

       1. File has exactly three newline-terminated lines.
       2. Line 1 (header) matches:
          ^TIMESTAMP\|URL\|STATUS_CODE\|RESULT\n$
       3. One of the remaining lines matches:
          ^[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2}T[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}Z\|http://127\.0\.0\.1:9090/ok\.json\|200\|SUCCESS\n$
       4. The other data line matches:
          ^[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2}T[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}Z\|http://127\.0\.0\.1:9090/missing\.json\|404\|FAILURE\n$

    C. Optional processes are not tested, but at grading time the
       http.server listening on :9090 may or may not still be running;
       only the presence and correctness of the log file is mandatory.

    <!-- Non-negotiable values the grader depends on -->
    • The log file must be named exactly “api_test_results.log”
      and live exactly in /home/user/connectivity_logs/
    • The delimiter character must be “|” (pipe).
    • SUCCESS only for 200, everything else is FAILURE.
